title: Breukencalculator
description: Voer optelling, aftrekking, vermenigvuldiging en deling van breuken uit. Resultaten in vereenvoudigde vorm.

faq.title: Veelgestelde Vragen
faq.q1: Hoe tel je breuken op met verschillende noemers?
faq.a1: Om breuken met verschillende noemers op te tellen, vind de kleinste gemene veelvoud (KGV) van de noemers. Converteer beide breuken naar de gemeenschappelijke noemer en tel de tellers op. Voorbeeld: 1/3 + 1/4. KGV(3,4) = 12. 4/12 + 3/12 = 7/12.
faq.a2: Om een breuk te vereenvoudigen, deel zowel de teller als de noemer door hun grootste gemene deler (GGD). Voorbeeld: 12/18. GGD(12,18) = 6. 12/6 = 2, 18/6 = 3. Vereenvoudigde vorm: 2/3.
faq.a3: Vermenigvuldig de tellers met elkaar en de noemers met elkaar: a/b × c/d = (a×c)/(b×d). Voorbeeld: 2/3 × 3/4 = 6/12 = 1/2.
faq.a4: Vermenigvuldig de eerste met het omgekeerde van de tweede: a/b ÷ c/d = a/b × d/c = (a×d)/(b×c). Voorbeeld: 3/4 ÷ 2/5 = 3/4 × 5/2 = 15/8 = 1 7/8.
faq.q5: Wat is een gemengd getal en hoe converteer je het naar een onechte breuk?
faq.a5: Een gemengd getal heeft een geheel deel en een breukdeel (bijv. 2 3/4). Om te converteren: vermenigvuldig het gehele deel met de noemer, tel de teller op, behoud de noemer. 2 3/4 = (2×4+3)/4 = 11/4.
faq.q6: Wat is de Kleinste Gemene Veelvoud (KGV)?
faq.a6: KGV van twee getallen is het kleinste getal deelbaar door beide. Methode: maak een lijst van veelvouden van elk getal totdat er een gemeenschappelijk is. KGV(4,6): veelvouden van 4: 4,8,12...; veelvouden van 6: 6,12...; KGV = 12.
faq.q7: Hoe converteer je breuken naar decimalen?
faq.a7: Deel de teller door de noemer: 3/4 = 3 ÷ 4 = 0,75. Sommige breuken geven eindige decimalen (1/4 = 0,25), andere periodieke decimalen (1/3 = 0,333...).
faq.q8: Hoe gebruik je breuken in kookrecepren?
faq.a8: Recepten gebruiken vaak breuken voor maten. Om een recept te verdubbelen, vermenigvuldig elke breukmoet met 2. Om te halveren, vermenigvuldig met 1/2. Voorbeeld: 2/3 kop × 2 = 4/3 = 1 1/3 kop.

content.whatIs:
A fraction calculator performs the four fundamental arithmetic operations — addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division — on two fractions, automatically simplifies the result to lowest terms, and displays the decimal equivalent. Enter any two fractions (proper, improper, or by entering whole numbers as numerators), select the operation, and get the exact result instantly with full simplification.

Fractions are one of the foundational structures of mathematics, predating decimal notation by thousands of years. Ancient Egyptians used unit fractions (fractions with numerator 1) for practical measurement and trade; ancient Babylonians used a base-60 fraction system from which we still derive our time units (60 minutes, 60 seconds). Today, fractions are indispensable in cooking, construction, engineering, finance, science, and music — anywhere precise ratios between quantities need to be expressed exactly rather than approximately as decimals.

The key advantage of fractions over decimals is exactness: 1/3 is exact, while 0.333... is an infinite non-terminating decimal. In any calculation chain where exact rational numbers matter — such as pharmaceutical dosing, engineering tolerances, or probability calculations — working in fractions preserves precision that decimal rounding would destroy. This calculator gives you the exact fractional result alongside the decimal approximation.
content.formula:
Addition:      a/b + c/d = (a×d + c×b) / (b×d)
Subtraction:   a/b − c/d = (a×d − c×b) / (b×d)
Multiplication: a/b × c/d = (a×c) / (b×d)
Division:      a/b ÷ c/d = a/b × d/c = (a×d) / (b×c)

Simplification — divide by GCD:
GCD(p, q) found via Euclidean algorithm:
  while q ≠ 0: (p, q) = (q, p mod q); return p
Simplified fraction: (p÷GCD) / (q÷GCD)

Mixed number conversion:
  Improper fraction p/q → whole: p÷q (integer division), remainder: p mod q
  Mixed number (n and r/q) → improper: (n×q + r)/q

Decimal = simplified numerator / simplified denominator
content.example:
Example 1 — Addition:
2/3 + 3/8
Common denominator: 3×8 = 24
Numerator: (2×8) + (3×3) = 16 + 9 = 25
Result: 25/24 (improper fraction)
GCD(25,24) = 1 → already simplified
Decimal: 25÷24 = 1.0417 (or 1 and 1/24 as mixed number)

Example 2 — Subtraction:
5/6 − 1/4
Numerator: (5×4) − (1×6) = 20 − 6 = 14
Denominator: 6×4 = 24
Result: 14/24 → GCD(14,24) = 2 → simplified: 7/12
Decimal: 7÷12 = 0.5833

Example 3 — Multiplication:
4/5 × 3/7
Numerator: 4×3 = 12
Denominator: 5×7 = 35
Result: 12/35 → GCD(12,35) = 1 → already simplified
Decimal: 0.3429

Example 4 — Division:
7/8 ÷ 3/4 = 7/8 × 4/3 = 28/24
GCD(28,24) = 4 → simplified: 7/6
Decimal: 1.1667 (or 1 and 1/6)
content.howItWorks_1: Enter the numerator and denominator for the first fraction. The numerator is the top number (parts counted); the denominator is the bottom number (total equal parts the whole is divided into). Negative numerators are accepted for negative fractions. Denominators must be non-zero — division by zero is undefined in mathematics.
content.howItWorks_2: Select the operation: Add (+), Subtract (−), Multiply (×), or Divide (÷). Then enter the numerator and denominator for the second fraction. For division, remember the calculator takes the reciprocal of the second fraction automatically — you do not need to do this yourself.
content.howItWorks_3: Click Calculate. The result appears as both a simplified fraction (numerator and denominator in lowest terms after GCD reduction) and as a decimal. If the result is an improper fraction (numerator greater than denominator), the decimal will be greater than 1. Use the decimal for quick comparison or further calculation; use the fraction form for exact representation.
content.whyItMatters:
Fraction arithmetic is not simply a school subject — it is the computational backbone of countless practical fields. In cooking, scaling a recipe from 4 servings to 6 requires multiplying every fractional ingredient measurement by 3/2 (or 1.5). Getting 2/3 cup × 3/2 wrong means the dish fails. In construction, cutting lumber or tile to fractional dimensions (3/8 inch, 7/16 inch) requires precise fraction subtraction and addition to minimize waste. In medicine, drug dosing calculations often involve fractions of standard doses adjusted for patient weight — an error here has direct consequences for patient safety.

In mathematics education, fluency with fraction arithmetic is the gateway to algebra, proportional reasoning, probability, and calculus. Research in mathematics education consistently shows that students who struggle with fraction concepts in middle school face persistent difficulties in algebra and higher mathematics. The National Mathematics Advisory Panel identified fraction proficiency as 'the most important foundational skill for algebra' — not because fractions are intrinsically more important than other topics, but because fraction arithmetic exercises every core skill needed for abstract mathematics: equivalence, ratio, inverse operations, and exact symbolic manipulation.

Professionally, fraction literacy distinguishes workers who can reason precisely from those who cannot. A nurse calculating medication drips, a carpenter calculating joint tolerances, a financial analyst computing fractional interest accruals, and a chef scaling a banquet recipe all rely on the same fraction arithmetic this calculator performs. The decimal approximation is useful; the exact fraction is necessary for precise work.
content.limitations:
This calculator performs operations on two fractions at a time. For expressions with more than two fractions (e.g., 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4), you must chain calculations: first compute 1/2 + 1/3 = 5/6, then compute 5/6 + 1/4 = 13/12. The result of the first calculation feeds into the second as a new input. This sequential approach is necessary because the calculator evaluates one binary operation at a time.

The calculator displays the result as an improper fraction if the numerator exceeds the denominator — it does not automatically convert to mixed number form. For example, 7/4 is displayed as 7/4 with decimal 1.75, not as '1 and 3/4'. To convert manually: whole part = 7÷4 = 1 (integer division), remainder = 7 mod 4 = 3, so 7/4 = 1 and 3/4. This is a display choice — the mathematical value is identical.

This calculator handles rational numbers (integers expressed as fractions) only. Irrational numbers expressed as fractions (such as √2/2 or π/4) cannot be entered directly as fractions since their numerators are not integers. For calculations involving irrational numbers, use a scientific calculator with decimal input.
content.tips_1: When adding or subtracting fractions by hand, use the LCD (Least Common Denominator) instead of the product of denominators to keep numbers smaller. The LCD of 4 and 6 is 12, not 24. Using the product (24) still works but produces a larger intermediate fraction that requires more simplification. This calculator uses the product-then-simplify approach internally, which is equivalent but you can verify results mentally using LCD.
content.tips_2: For fraction division, always use 'keep, change, flip': Keep the first fraction unchanged, Change division to multiplication, Flip (reciprocate) the second fraction. This is the standard algorithm: a/b ÷ c/d = a/b × d/c. The mathematical justification: multiplying by d/c is the same as dividing by c/d, because (c/d) × (d/c) = 1.
content.tips_3: Cross-cancellation before multiplying fractions simplifies the calculation. Before multiplying 4/9 × 3/8: check if any numerator shares a common factor with any denominator. 4 and 8 share factor 4 (4÷4=1, 8÷4=2). 3 and 9 share factor 3 (3÷3=1, 9÷3=3). So: (4/9) × (3/8) = (1/3) × (1/2) = 1/6. Much simpler than 12/72 → GCD → 1/6.
content.tips_4: Converting between fractions and percentages is a daily skill: percentage = fraction × 100. Common equivalents to memorize: 1/2 = 50%, 1/3 ≈ 33.3%, 1/4 = 25%, 3/4 = 75%, 1/5 = 20%, 1/8 = 12.5%, 3/8 = 37.5%, 5/8 = 62.5%, 7/8 = 87.5%. These appear constantly in discounts, statistics, and proportional reasoning. The decimal output from this calculator × 100 = percentage.
